title: Structures, powers, and functions of Congress: advanced
description: Practice your skills in this exercise about the structures, powers, and functions of Congress.
topic_path: What powers does Congress have in the U.S. government?
Structures, powers, and functions of Congress: advanced

assessment_items.question_content:
Representative Siddiqui introduces a bill that would reform campaign finance in the House of Representatives. 

**After the bill is assigned a number, which of the following describes the next step of the lawmaking process?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Speaker of the House assigns the bill to a relevant committee
The bill is sent to the floor of the House to be voted on
The Senate receives the bill to add amendments
The Majority Leader of the House schedules a hearing

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements explains how the structural differences between the House and Senate affect the policymaking process? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Because members of the House have shorter term lengths, they are more likely to be influenced by demands from their constituents
Because members of the Senate have longer term lengths, they are more likely to be influenced by demands from their constituents
Because members of the House have shorter term lengths, the House has weaker and less structured leadership than the Senate
Because members of the Senate have longer term lengths, the Senate has a stronger and more structured leadership than the House

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements best explains an advantage the majority party in the House of Representatives has in lawmaking?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It can assign bills to committees
It can place holds on bills on the floor
It nominates people for bureaucratic positions
It becomes the majority party in the Senate

assessment_items.question_content:
The president wants Congress to pass a new tax to help raise funds for a high-speed transcontinental train. 

**Which of the following is the first step in the process of passing the bill?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A member of the House must introduce the bill to the House floor
A member of the Senate must introduce the bill to the Senate floor
A member of either the House or Senate can propose the bill
The Speaker of the House sends the bill to committee

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an accurate comparison of the structure and function of the House of Representatives and the Senate?**

 | House of Representatives | Senate
- | - | -
**A** | Members can delay the policymaking process through the use of a filibuster | Members can delay the policymaking process through the use of holds
**B** | Responsible for proposing all tax bills | Responsible for ratifying all treaties
**C** | Constituents of an entire state choose elected members| Constituents in a local district choose elected members
**D** | Responsible for initiating all articles of impeachment | Responsible for scheduling bills for the floor of the House

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Comparison A
Comparison B
Comparison C
Comparison D

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is a consequence of holds applied to a bill in the Senate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A senator can slow down the legislative process
A senator can force the bill into committee to be reconsidered
A senator can put the bill up for a vote for unanimous consent
A senator can expedite the legislative process by limiting debate

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an accurate comparison of the roles of President of the Senate and Speaker of the House?**

 | President of the Senate | Speaker of the House
- | - | -
**A** | Manages the Rules Committee | Oversees day-to-day operation of the legislative process
**B** | Persuades members to support the majority party’s position | Serves as national spokesperson for the party
**C** | Elected by the minority party of the Senate | Elected by the majority party of the House 
**D** | Casts a vote only if there is a tie | Assigns legislation to committees

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Comparison A
Comparison B
Comparison C
Comparison D

assessment_items.question_content:
The majority party in the Senate wants to vote on a bill that would change requirements for granting citizenship, but a member of the minority party has the floor and continues to debate the bill. 

**Which of the following may a senator of the majority party do to stop debate on the bill?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Propose a cloture vote
Start to filibuster
Set a time limit on the floor debate
File a discharge petition

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following describes the role of the House Rules Committee?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Schedules and manages the flow of legislation on the floor
Reconciles differences between House and Senate versions of a bill
Sponsors all budget appropriation bills
Determines bureaucratic regulations after a bill becomes a law

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements best explains how rule differences in the House of Representatives and the Senate can affect the policymaking process?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Even though the House may pass a bill, the Senate can kill the bill with a filibuster
Even though the Senate may pass a bill, the House can delay it with a hold
Even though the House may pass a bill, the Senate Rules Committee can delay it
Even though the Senate may pass a bill, the House can delay the bill with a discharge petition

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8b6c76a885ccc6097384172f2a9b6b7b.png)
Source: Boris Rasin, “Axe or Scalpel” political cartoon, [Flickr creative commons](https://www.flickr.com/photos/58551403@N06/5528424516/)

**Which of the following statements is most consistent with the cartoon's message?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: As entitlement costs grow, discretionary spending has to decrease unless tax revenues increase or Congress permits the budget deficit to increase
As discretionary spending grows, entitlement costs have to increase to maintain the budget
As discretionary spending grows, members of both the Republican party and Democratic party seek to cut entitlement costs
As entitlement costs grow, discretionary spending has to increase in order to reduce the budget deficit

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/345b4cb7d704f8b7f474dd9731cc0107.svg)

**Which of the following accurately summarizes the information presented in the pie charts?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Entitlement spending has steadily become a larger portion of the federal budget
From 1973 to 1993, discretionary spending increased more than entitlement spending
Mandatory spending is composed of discretionary spending plus net interest
Discretionary spending and entitlements have caused the federal budget to grow

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/345b4cb7d704f8b7f474dd9731cc0107.svg)

**Based on the information in the pie charts, which of the following is the most likely implication of entitlement and discretionary spending as a portion of the federal budget?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: As entitlement costs grow, discretionary spending opportunities will decrease
As discretionary spending decreases, entitlement costs will also decrease
Entitlement costs will remain unchanged due to laws capping program outlays
Discretionary spending will increase because both parties agree on funding these programs

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/345b4cb7d704f8b7f474dd9731cc0107.svg)

**Which of the following programs would fall under the discretionary spending portion of the pie chart?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Defense spending
Social security
Medicare funding
Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Pork barrel legislation is best defined as which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Laws that include federally-funded local projects that benefit a specific district
Laws that include provisions on how agriculture and farming should be conducted
Laws that die in committees because they are unpopular
Laws that are created by the president without congressional approval

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios would be considered logrolling?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A senator agrees to vote on another senator’s bill, provided that the senator will support their own legislation
A senator holds the floor of the Senate, refusing to give it up and preventing a vote on the bill
A senator proposes a plan to redraw the geographic boundaries of legislative districts in their state to support one political party over the other party
A senator adds a federally-funded project that benefits their district to a bill that will be passed through Congress

assessment_items.question_content:
Senator Jon Snow has proposed a bill to increase funds to the military to fight the White Walkers threatening to invade the North. He hasn’t received enough support to get the bill to pass, so he reaches out to Senator Daenerys Targaryen for her support. She agrees to support his bill as long as Senator Snow supports her legislation that allows public schools to teach High Valyrian as an elective language.

**This is an example of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Logrolling
Filibuster
Pork barrel legislation
Gerrymandering

assessment_items.question_content:
Congress member Chidi Anagonye has proposed an Agriculture Appropriations bill that would fund important agricultural food programs and food services. While the bill was in committee, Congress member Eleanor Shellstrop added an amendment to the bill that would send \$250,000 to her district in Arizona for an Arizona Fried Shrimp Institute. She says she will only vote for the bill if it includes the funds for her district.

**This is an example of which of the following?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Pork barrel legislation
Logrolling
Filibuster
Gerrymandering
